title: Reading and Use of English – Part 5
description: You are going to read a magazine article about an intellectual process known as critical thinking. For questions 31 – 36, choose the answer (A, B, C or D) which you think fits best according to the text. Mark your answers on the separate answer sheet.

content.title: The Philosophy of Time in a Fast-Paced World
content.subtitle: How our perception of the 'present' is being distorted by modern chronopolitics.
content.text: Time, in the classical Newtonian sense, is an absolute—a steady, unceasing river that flows at the same rate for everyone. However, the lived experience of time in the 21st century suggests something far more fragmented. We are living in an era of 'chronopolitics,' where the speed at which one can process information or respond to market changes has become a primary form of power. [31] The writer suggests that this 'acceleration of life' is not just a technological byproduct, but a psychological burden that forces us into a state of 'permanent presentism,' where the past is forgotten and the future is only a series of urgent deadlines. [/31] || This shift has profound implications for our capacity for deep thought. In the past, the rhythm of life allowed for 'temporal buffers'—the time spent walking to a meeting, waiting for a letter, or simply sitting in silence. These buffers provided the mental space required for reflection and the synthesis of complex ideas. [32] Today, these gaps are increasingly filled by 'micro-tasks'—checking a phone, responding to a text, or scrolling through a feed—effectively eliminating the 'fallow periods' that the human brain needs to remain creative. [/32] We have become incredibly efficient at 'reacting' to time, but we have lost the ability to 'inhabit' it. || [33] Interestingly, some philosophers argue that our current obsession with 'saving time' through automation and efficiency is actually making us feel more rushed than ever. [/33] This is the 'Efficiency Paradox': as we find ways to do tasks faster, we do not use the extra time to rest. Instead, we simply fill the space with more tasks. The result is a society that is technically more efficient but psychologically more exhausted. The 'saved' time is never actually realized; it is immediately reinvested into the economy of constant production, leaving us like the Red Queen in Alice in Wonderland, running faster and faster just to stay in the same place. || The author also explores the concept of 'Social Acceleration.' It is not just our gadgets that are faster; our social institutions are also under pressure to keep up. [34] This leads to a 'crisis of democracy,' where the slow, deliberative processes required for sound policy-making are viewed as obsolete obstacles to the rapid-fire demands of the digital electorate. [/34] When we prioritize speed over deliberation, we risk making short-term decisions that have long-term catastrophic consequences, from environmental degradation to financial instability. We are sacrificing the 'long view' for the 'immediate hit.' || [35] To counter this, some have turned to the 'Slow Movement,' advocating for a deliberate deceleration of various aspects of life, from food to education. [/35] Critics often dismiss this as a nostalgic retreat into the past, but proponents argue it is a radical act of reclamation. It is about choosing 'tempo' over 'speed'—recognizing that while some things should be fast, the most important things in life (like learning, grief, and love) require their own organic, unhurried pace. It is an attempt to align our biological clocks with our technological ones, rather than the other way around. || Ultimately, the way we perceive time defines our relationship with the world. [36] The writer concludes that the ultimate challenge of the modern age is to find 'islands of stillness' in the sea of acceleration. [/36] It is only by stepping out of the stream of constant distraction that we can regain our agency and move from being 'subjects of time' to being its masters. The future belongs not to those who can move the fastest, but to those who know when to stop and look at where they are going.

questions.question: In the first paragraph, what does the writer suggest about 'permanent presentism'?
options: A) It is a positive state where we focus only on what truly matters.
B) It is a result of having too much free time in the modern era.
C) It is a psychological state that prevents us from thinking about the past or future.
D) It is a new form of political power that allows for faster decision-making.
questions.explanation: C is supported by [31], stating it is a 'psychological burden' where the past is forgotten and the future is just deadlines. A is the opposite of the author's tone. B is contradicted. D confuses the cause ('chronopolitics') with the psychological result.

questions.question: The second paragraph implies that 'temporal buffers' were important because they...
options: A) allowed people to complete more tasks in a single day.
B) gave the brain the necessary time for deep reflection.
C) were the only moments when people could use technology.
D) helped people communicate more quickly with one another.
questions.explanation: B is supported by [32] and the mention of 'mental space required for reflection.' A is the opposite of a 'buffer.' C and D are not supported by the text.

questions.question: What is the 'Efficiency Paradox' mentioned in the third paragraph?
options: A) The more efficient we become, the more free time we have to rest.
B) Technology that is supposed to save time actually increases our workload.
C) Automated systems are less efficient than manual processes in the long run.
D) People who work fast are usually less productive than those who work slow.
questions.explanation: B is supported by [33] and the explanation that 'as we find ways to do tasks faster... we simply fill the space with more tasks.' A is the ideal that the paradox proves wrong. C and D are not the focus.

questions.question: According to the fourth paragraph, 'Social Acceleration' can lead to a 'crisis of democracy' because...
options: A) people no longer have time to go to the polls to vote.
B) digital systems are too complex for most politicians to understand.
C) careful and slow decision-making is seen as an unnecessary delay.
D) the electorate is more interested in environmental issues than in speed.
questions.explanation: C is supported by [34] and the mention of slow processes being viewed as 'obsolete obstacles.' A and B are not mentioned. D is an example of what might be lost, not a cause of the crisis.

questions.question: The writer suggests that the 'Slow Movement' is...
options: A) an attempt to stop all technological progress completely.
B) a way to ensure that all tasks are performed as slowly as possible.
C) a radical effort to prioritize personal rhythms over technological ones.
D) a nostalgic trend that is irrelevant to the modern workplace.
questions.explanation: C is supported by [35] and the idea of aligning 'biological clocks with our technological ones.' A and B are exaggerations. D is a criticism that the author then argues against.

questions.question: Which of the following best describes the writer's conclusion in the final paragraph?
options: A) We must learn to move even faster to survive in the future economy.
B) Agency is found by finding moments of quiet amidst the constant rush.
C) Time is an absolute reality that cannot be changed by human perception.
D) Stop using all digital devices is the only way to become a 'master of time'.
questions.explanation: B is supported by [36] and the call for 'islands of stillness.' A is the opposite of the conclusion. C refers to the Newtonian view mentioned at the start. D is an extreme solution not stated in the text.
